title: RBAC: Least-Privilege Pod Reader
objective: Create a namespace-scoped Role that only allows reading pods, bind it to a ServiceAccount, and verify the permissions are correctly scoped.
context: A monitoring application needs to list and inspect pods in the 'monitoring' namespace but must NOT be able to create, delete, or access secrets. This is a core RBAC least-privilege scenario tested on KCSA.
tasks: Create a namespace called 'monitoring'
Create a ServiceAccount named 'pod-monitor' in the 'monitoring' namespace
Create a Role named 'pod-reader' in 'monitoring' that allows: get, list, watch on pods
Create a RoleBinding named 'pod-monitor-binding' that binds the 'pod-reader' Role to the 'pod-monitor' ServiceAccount
Verify the ServiceAccount CAN list pods but CANNOT list secrets or create pods
hints: Use 'kubectl create role pod-reader --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods -n monitoring'
Use 'kubectl create rolebinding' or write YAML for more control
Test permissions with: kubectl auth can-i <verb> <resource> --as=system:serviceaccount:monitoring:pod-monitor -n monitoring
solution:
kubectl create namespace monitoring
kubectl create serviceaccount pod-monitor -n monitoring
kubectl create role pod-reader --verb=get,list,watch --resource=pods -n monitoring
kubectl create rolebinding pod-monitor-binding --role=pod-reader --serviceaccount=monitoring:pod-monitor -n monitoring
validation_commands: kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=system:serviceaccount:monitoring:pod-monitor -n monitoring
kubectl auth can-i list secrets --as=system:serviceaccount:monitoring:pod-monitor -n monitoring
kubectl auth can-i create pods --as=system:serviceaccount:monitoring:pod-monitor -n monitoring
kubectl auth can-i list pods --as=system:serviceaccount:monitoring:pod-monitor -n default
key_concepts: Roles are namespace-scoped; ClusterRoles are cluster-scoped
RoleBinding binds within one namespace; ClusterRoleBinding binds cluster-wide
system:serviceaccount:<namespace>:<name> is the full identity format for service accounts
kubectl auth can-i is your best friend for verifying RBAC

title: NetworkPolicy: Default Deny with Selective Allow
objective: Implement a default-deny NetworkPolicy and then selectively allow traffic between specific pods using label selectors.
context: By default, all pods can communicate freely. A proper security posture starts with deny-all and explicitly opens only required paths. This is a critical KCSA concept — understanding that pods without any NetworkPolicy are non-isolated.
prerequisites: minikube start --cni=calico  (CNI must support NetworkPolicy; use calico or cilium)
tasks: Create a namespace 'netpol-demo'
Deploy a pod named 'frontend' with label 'app=frontend' using image nginx:alpine
Deploy a pod named 'backend' with label 'app=backend' using image nginx:alpine
Deploy a pod named 'database' with label 'app=database' using image nginx:alpine
Apply a default-deny-all ingress NetworkPolicy to the 'netpol-demo' namespace
Apply a NetworkPolicy that allows 'frontend' to reach 'backend' on port 80
Apply a NetworkPolicy that allows 'backend' to reach 'database' on port 80
Verify: frontend->backend allowed, frontend->database blocked, backend->database allowed
hints: Default deny policy uses empty podSelector: {} to select ALL pods and empty ingress: [] or no ingress spec
Use 'kubectl exec <pod> -- wget -qO- --timeout=2 <pod-ip>' to test connectivity
Get pod IPs with: kubectl get pods -n netpol-demo -o wide
NetworkPolicy podSelector matches pods in the SAME namespace by default
solution:
# Default deny all ingress in namespace
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: default-deny-ingress
  namespace: netpol-demo
spec:
  podSelector: {}
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
---
# Allow frontend -> backend
apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: NetworkPolicy
metadata:
  name: allow-frontend-to-backend
  namespace: netpol-demo
spec:
  podSelector:
    matchLabels:
      app: backend
  policyTypes:
  - Ingress
  ingress:
  - from:
    - podSelector:
        matchLabels:
          app: frontend
    ports:
    - port: 80
validation_commands: kubectl exec -n netpol-demo frontend -- wget -qO- --timeout=2 http://<backend-ip>  # should succeed
kubectl exec -n netpol-demo frontend -- wget -qO- --timeout=2 http://<database-ip> # should timeout
kubectl exec -n netpol-demo backend  -- wget -qO- --timeout=2 http://<database-ip> # should succeed (if policy applied)
key_concepts: Pods are non-isolated by default — NetworkPolicy only restricts selected pods
Empty podSelector {} selects ALL pods in the namespace
NetworkPolicy is additive — multiple policies combine (union of allowed traffic)
Egress policies restrict outgoing traffic; Ingress policies restrict incoming traffic
CNI plugin must support NetworkPolicy (Calico, Cilium, Weave — NOT Flannel by default)

title: Pod Security: Hardened SecurityContext
objective: Deploy a pod with a hardened securityContext that implements the 'restricted' Pod Security Standard requirements.
context: The restricted PSS is the most secure profile. Understanding each field and WHY it matters is KCSA exam content. You will apply securityContext settings at both pod and container level.
tasks: Create a namespace 'pss-demo' with the label to enforce the restricted Pod Security Standard
Try to deploy a simple nginx pod — observe that it gets rejected

Deploy a hardened pod that satisfies the restricted standard with: runAsNonRoot, runAsUser: 1000, readOnlyRootFilesystem, drop ALL capabilities, allowPrivilegeEscalation: false, seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault
Verify the pod is running and check the security context is applied
Exec into the pod and confirm you are running as user 1000, not root
hints: Label the namespace: kubectl label namespace pss-demo pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted
nginx:alpine runs as root by default — use 'nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged' instead
readOnlyRootFilesystem requires you to mount tmpfs for any writable paths the app needs
The seccompProfile is set at the pod level: spec.securityContext.seccompProfile.type: RuntimeDefault
solution:
# Label namespace
kubectl create namespace pss-demo
kubectl label namespace pss-demo pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted

# Hardened pod manifest
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: hardened-pod
  namespace: pss-demo
spec:
  securityContext:
    runAsNonRoot: true
    runAsUser: 1000
    seccompProfile:
      type: RuntimeDefault
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:alpine
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      readOnlyRootFilesystem: true
      capabilities:
        drop:
        - ALL
    volumeMounts:
    - name: tmp
      mountPath: /tmp
    - name: cache
      mountPath: /var/cache/nginx
    - name: run
      mountPath: /var/run
  volumes:
  - name: tmp
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: cache
    emptyDir: {}
  - name: run
    emptyDir: {}
validation_commands: kubectl describe pod hardened-pod -n pss-demo | grep -A 20 'Security Context'
key_concepts: Pod-level securityContext applies to all containers; container-level overrides per-container
runAsNonRoot: true rejects pods where the image user is root (UID 0)
readOnlyRootFilesystem prevents writes to the container filesystem — use emptyDir/PVC for writable dirs
capabilities.drop: [ALL] then selectively add back only what's needed
allowPrivilegeEscalation: false prevents setuid/setgid binaries from gaining more privileges
seccompProfile: RuntimeDefault uses the container runtime's default syscall filter

title: Secrets: Secure Creation and Consumption
objective: Create Kubernetes Secrets and consume them securely as environment variables and as mounted files, understanding the security trade-offs of each approach.
context: Secrets are base64-encoded, not encrypted by default. Understanding how to create them without exposing values in shell history, and the difference between env var and volume mount consumption, is KCSA content.
tasks: Create a Secret named 'db-credentials' from literal values (username=admin, password=supersecret) WITHOUT using --from-literal in a way that exposes it in shell history (use a file or env var)
Create a pod that consumes the username as an environment variable and the password as a mounted file
Verify the secret is mounted correctly and the values are accessible
Show the 'risk': decode the base64 value directly from the Secret object
Disable automountServiceAccountToken on the pod's ServiceAccount
hints: To avoid shell history: create the secret from a file ('kubectl create secret generic ... --from-file=password=./password.txt') or use --from-env-file
Volume mount for secrets is more secure than env vars — env vars can be logged by crash dumps
kubectl get secret db-credentials -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d
Set automountServiceAccountToken: false on the ServiceAccount, not just the Pod
solution:
# Create secret from file (avoids shell history)
echo -n 'supersecret' > /tmp/password.txt
echo -n 'admin' > /tmp/username.txt
kubectl create secret generic db-credentials \
  --from-file=username=/tmp/username.txt \
  --from-file=password=/tmp/password.txt
rm /tmp/password.txt /tmp/username.txt

# Pod that consumes the secret
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secret-consumer
spec:
  automountServiceAccountToken: false
  containers:
  - name: app
    image: busybox
    command: ['sh', '-c', 'echo Username: $DB_USER && cat /secrets/password && sleep 3600']
    env:
    - name: DB_USER
      valueFrom:
        secretKeyRef:
          name: db-credentials
          key: username
    volumeMounts:
    - name: secret-vol
      mountPath: /secrets
      readOnly: true
  volumes:
  - name: secret-vol
    secret:
      secretName: db-credentials
      items:
      - key: password
        path: password
        mode: 0400
validation_commands: kubectl exec secret-consumer -- env | grep DB_USER
kubectl get secret db-credentials -o jsonpath='{.data.password}' | base64 -d
cleanup: kubectl delete pod secret-consumer && kubectl delete secret db-credentials
key_concepts: Secrets are base64-encoded, NOT encrypted — anyone with 'get secret' permission can decode them
Volume mounts are preferred over env vars — env vars can appear in logs, crash dumps, and child processes
Set mode: 0400 on mounted secret files to prevent world-readable access
automountServiceAccountToken: false is a best practice for pods that don't call the Kubernetes API
Use encryption at rest (EncryptionConfiguration) and RBAC to protect Secrets in etcd

title: Audit Logging: Enable and Analyze Audit Events
objective: Configure Kubernetes audit logging on minikube, perform some actions, and analyze the audit log to trace who did what.
context: Audit logs are critical for detecting unauthorized access and post-incident forensics. The KCSA exam tests understanding of audit policy levels, stages, and what gets captured. On minikube, you configure this via extra flags.
prerequisites: Understand: audit stages are RequestReceived, ResponseStarted, ResponseComplete, Panic
tasks: Create an audit policy file that: logs Secret access at Metadata level, logs pod creation at RequestResponse level, and logs everything else at Metadata level
Start minikube with audit logging enabled pointing to your policy file
Perform actions: create a secret, create a pod, list nodes
Examine the audit log to find the events for each action
Identify the user, verb, resource, and response code in each event
hints: Audit policy file location: create at /tmp/audit-policy.yaml then pass to minikube

minikube start --extra-config=apiserver.audit-policy-file=/etc/ssl/certs/audit-policy.yaml --extra-config=apiserver.audit-log-path=/var/log/kubernetes/audit.log --mount-string=/tmp/audit-policy.yaml:/etc/ssl/certs/audit-policy.yaml --mount
Read logs from minikube: minikube ssh 'sudo cat /var/log/kubernetes/audit.log' | head -50
Each audit event is a JSON line — use 'jq' to parse: ... | jq '{user:.user.username, verb:.verb, resource:.objectRef.resource}'
solution:
# /tmp/audit-policy.yaml
apiVersion: audit.k8s.io/v1
kind: Policy
rules:
# Log secret access at Metadata (don't log values)
- level: Metadata
  resources:
  - group: ''
    resources: ['secrets']
# Log pod mutations at RequestResponse
- level: RequestResponse
  verbs: ['create', 'update', 'delete']
  resources:
  - group: ''
    resources: ['pods']
# Catch-all: Metadata for everything else
- level: Metadata

# Start minikube
minikube start \
  --mount \
  --mount-string=/tmp/audit-policy.yaml:/etc/ssl/certs/audit-policy.yaml \
  --extra-config=apiserver.audit-policy-file=/etc/ssl/certs/audit-policy.yaml \
  --extra-config=apiserver.audit-log-path=/var/log/kubernetes/audit.log

# View logs
minikube ssh 'sudo tail -f /var/log/kubernetes/audit.log' | jq '{user:.user.username, verb:.verb, resource:.objectRef.resource, code:.responseStatus.code}'
validation_commands: minikube ssh 'sudo cat /var/log/kubernetes/audit.log' | grep 'secrets' | tail -3 | jq '{user:.user.username,verb:.verb,level:.level}'
key_concepts: Audit levels: None, Metadata, Request, RequestResponse — more verbose = more storage cost
Log Secrets at Metadata level — RequestResponse would log the secret VALUE
Audit stages: RequestReceived (immediately), ResponseStarted (for streaming), ResponseComplete, Panic
Audit logs are critical for compliance (SOC2, PCI-DSS require access logging to sensitive data)
Use a catch-all rule at the end of the policy as a safety net

title: Runtime Security: Privileged vs Restricted Container
objective: Understand the security impact of privileged containers by comparing a privileged and a non-privileged container's access to the host system.
context: Privileged containers are a major security risk — they have nearly full access to the host kernel. This lab demonstrates WHY privileged mode is dangerous and should be blocked by policy.
tasks: Deploy a privileged pod and check what it can access on the host (list block devices, see host processes)
Deploy a non-privileged pod and attempt the same operations — observe the failures
Try to load a kernel module from inside the privileged container
Apply a Pod Security Standard 'restricted' namespace label and try to create the privileged pod — observe the rejection
hints: privileged: true is set in spec.containers[].securityContext
List block devices in privileged: ls /dev/sd* or lsblk
See host processes in privileged: nsenter --target 1 --mount --uts --ipc --net --pid -- ps aux
Non-privileged containers see only their own processes (PID 1 = their entrypoint)
solution:
# Privileged pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: privileged-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: priv
    image: alpine
    command: ['sleep', '3600']
    securityContext:
      privileged: true
---
# Non-privileged pod
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: unprivileged-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: unpriv
    image: alpine
    command: ['sleep', '3600']
    securityContext:
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities:
        drop: [ALL]
validation_commands: kubectl create namespace restricted-test && kubectl label namespace restricted-test pod-security.kubernetes.io/enforce=restricted && kubectl run priv-blocked -n restricted-test --image=alpine --overrides='{"spec":{"containers":[{"name":"c","image":"alpine","securityContext":{"privileged":true}}]}}' 2>&1
cleanup: kubectl delete pod privileged-pod unprivileged-pod && kubectl delete namespace restricted-test
key_concepts: privileged: true gives the container near-root access to the host kernel
A compromised privileged container can escape to the host node
The 'restricted' PSS blocks privileged: true and many other dangerous settings
OPA Gatekeeper or Kyverno can also enforce no-privileged-containers at cluster level
Even without privileged: true, a container with certain capabilities (NET_ADMIN, SYS_ADMIN) can be dangerous

title: Image Security: Scan with Trivy and Set Pull Policy
objective: Scan container images for vulnerabilities using Trivy and understand how to enforce secure image practices in Kubernetes.
context: Image scanning is the first line of defense against known vulnerabilities. KCSA tests knowledge of CVE scanning, image hardening, and Kubernetes image pull policies.
prerequisites: Install Trivy: https://aquasecurity.github.io/trivy/latest/getting-started/installation/ (brew install aquasecurity/trivy/trivy OR scoop install trivy)
tasks: Scan the 'nginx:latest' image with Trivy — identify how many HIGH and CRITICAL CVEs it has
Scan 'nginx:alpine' and compare the CVE count
Scan 'nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:alpine' and note the improvement
Deploy a pod with imagePullPolicy: Always and a pinned digest instead of a mutable tag
Use 'kubectl describe pod' to verify the image digest that was pulled
hints: trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL nginx:latest
trivy image --format table nginx:alpine
Pin an image by digest: nginx@sha256:<digest>  — get digest with: docker inspect nginx:alpine | jq '.[0].RepoDigests'
OR: minikube ssh 'docker inspect nginx:alpine' to get digest inside minikube VM
solution:
# Scan commands
trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL nginx:latest
trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL nginx:alpine
trivy image --severity HIGH,CRITICAL nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:alpine

# Get image digest
DIGEST=$(docker manifest inspect nginx:alpine | jq -r '.config.digest')

# Pod with pinned image and Always pull policy
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: secure-image-pod
spec:
  containers:
  - name: web
    image: nginx:alpine  # In prod: use @sha256:<digest>
    imagePullPolicy: Always
    securityContext:
      runAsNonRoot: true
      runAsUser: 101
      allowPrivilegeEscalation: false
      capabilities:
        drop: [ALL]
validation_commands: kubectl describe pod secure-image-pod | grep 'Image ID'
key_concepts: Minimal base images (alpine, distroless) dramatically reduce CVE surface area
Trivy scans OS packages AND language-specific dependencies (pip, npm, go.sum, etc.)
imagePullPolicy: Always forces the registry check — prevents stale cached images
Pinning by digest (@sha256:...) is cryptographically immutable — unlike tags which can be overwritten
Integrate Trivy into CI/CD to fail builds when CRITICAL CVEs are found

title: OPA Gatekeeper: Policy as Code
objective: Install OPA Gatekeeper and create a policy that requires all pods to have resource limits defined.
context: Gatekeeper uses ValidatingAdmissionWebhook to enforce custom policies written in Rego. This prevents configuration drift and enforces security standards at admission time — before objects are persisted.
tasks: Install OPA Gatekeeper using the official manifest
Create a ConstraintTemplate that defines a policy requiring all containers to have CPU and memory limits
Create a Constraint (instance of the template) that enforces this across all namespaces except kube-system
Try to create a pod WITHOUT resource limits — observe the rejection
Create a pod WITH proper resource limits — verify it succeeds
Use the Gatekeeper audit feature to find existing violations
hints: Install: kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/open-policy-agent/gatekeeper/master/deploy/gatekeeper.yaml
ConstraintTemplate defines the Rego logic; Constraint is the 'instance' that applies it
The Rego package must be named: package k8srequiredlimits (match your CRD name)
Audit results appear in the Constraint object: kubectl describe k8srequiredlimits require-limits
solution:
# ConstraintTemplate
apiVersion: templates.gatekeeper.sh/v1
kind: ConstraintTemplate
metadata:
  name: k8srequiredlimits
spec:
  crd:
    spec:
      names:
        kind: K8sRequiredLimits
  targets:
  - target: admission.k8s.gatekeeper.sh
    rego: |
      package k8srequiredlimits
      violation[{"msg": msg}] {
        container := input.review.object.spec.containers[_]
        not container.resources.limits.cpu
        msg := sprintf("Container '%v' must have CPU limits", [container.name])
      }
      violation[{"msg": msg}] {
        container := input.review.object.spec.containers[_]
        not container.resources.limits.memory
        msg := sprintf("Container '%v' must have memory limits", [container.name])
      }
---
# Constraint instance
apiVersion: constraints.gatekeeper.sh/v1beta1
kind: K8sRequiredLimits
metadata:
  name: require-limits
spec:
  match:
    kinds:
    - apiGroups: [""]
      kinds: ["Pod"]
    excludedNamespaces: ["kube-system", "gatekeeper-system"]
validation_commands: kubectl describe k8srequiredlimits require-limits | grep -A 5 'Total Violations'
cleanup: kubectl delete k8srequiredlimits require-limits && kubectl delete constrainttemplate k8srequiredlimits
key_concepts: OPA Gatekeeper uses ValidatingAdmissionWebhook — requests are evaluated BEFORE persistence
ConstraintTemplate defines the Rego policy logic; Constraint is an instance with scope/parameters
Gatekeeper has an Audit mode — periodically checks existing resources for violations
Rego is a declarative policy language — violations[] defines what's disallowed
Kyverno is a Kubernetes-native alternative to Gatekeeper that uses YAML policies instead of Rego

title: mTLS with Service Mesh: Istio Basics
objective: Install Istio on minikube, enable automatic mTLS, and verify that pod-to-pod traffic is encrypted and authenticated.
context: Service meshes like Istio implement mTLS transparently via sidecar injection. This enforces Zero Trust networking — every service must present a valid certificate. KCSA tests understanding of mTLS, service mesh concepts, and PeerAuthentication policies.
tasks: Install Istio with the demo profile on minikube
Enable automatic sidecar injection for a namespace 'mtls-demo'
Deploy two services: 'client' and 'server' — verify Envoy sidecars are injected
Apply a PeerAuthentication policy requiring STRICT mTLS in the 'mtls-demo' namespace
Verify traffic works between client and server (mTLS transparent)
Try to connect from a pod WITHOUT a sidecar — verify it's blocked by STRICT mode
Use 'istioctl authn tls-check' to verify mTLS status
hints: Enable injection: kubectl label namespace mtls-demo istio-injection=enabled
Check sidecars: kubectl get pod -n mtls-demo -o jsonpath='{.items[*].spec.containers[*].name}'
PeerAuthentication with mode: STRICT requires mTLS for ALL communication to selected pods
istioctl authn tls-check <pod-name> shows the mTLS status for that pod's connections
solution:
# Label namespace for injection
kubectl create namespace mtls-demo
kubectl label namespace mtls-demo istio-injection=enabled

# Deploy client and server
kubectl run server -n mtls-demo --image=nginxinc/nginx-unprivileged:alpine
kubectl run client -n mtls-demo --image=curlimages/curl -- sleep 3600

# PeerAuthentication - STRICT mTLS
apiVersion: security.istio.io/v1beta1
kind: PeerAuthentication
metadata:
  name: strict-mtls
  namespace: mtls-demo
spec:
  mtls:
    mode: STRICT

# Verify
kubectl exec -n mtls-demo client -- curl -s http://server/
istioctl authn tls-check client.mtls-demo
validation_commands: kubectl exec -n mtls-demo client -- curl -s -o /dev/null -w '%{http_code}' http://server/
istioctl authn tls-check client.mtls-demo.svc.cluster.local 2>/dev/null | grep mtls-demo
expected_outputs: client: curl istio-proxy, server: nginx istio-proxy
key_concepts: mTLS authenticates BOTH client and server — neither can be an impersonator
Istio injects Envoy sidecar proxies that handle mTLS transparently (app code unchanged)
PeerAuthentication STRICT mode rejects non-mTLS connections (enforces Zero Trust)
PERMISSIVE mode accepts both mTLS and plain text — useful during migration
Istio's SPIFFE-based identity (spiffe://cluster.local/ns/NS/sa/SA) is the workload certificate identity

title: CIS Benchmark: Cluster Security Audit with kube-bench
objective: Run kube-bench against your minikube cluster to identify CIS Kubernetes Benchmark violations and understand the remediation steps.
context: kube-bench automates CIS Benchmark checks — the same checks used by compliance auditors for SOC2, PCI-DSS, and other frameworks. Understanding how to run it and interpret results is direct KCSA exam content.
tasks: Run kube-bench inside the minikube cluster as a Kubernetes Job
Examine the results — identify FAIL and WARN items
Find the check for: kubelet read-only port, API server anonymous auth, etcd encryption
Document 3 FAIL items and their remediation steps
Understand why some checks are INFO or WARN vs FAIL
hints: Run kube-bench as a Job: kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/kube-bench/main/job.yaml
View results: kubectl logs job/kube-bench
Look for sections: [FAIL], [WARN], [PASS], [INFO]
kube-bench auto-detects Kubernetes version and applies the appropriate CIS benchmark version
Some checks are marked WARN not FAIL because they depend on your specific environment/use case
solution:
# Run kube-bench as a Job
kubectl apply -f https://raw.githubusercontent.com/aquasecurity/kube-bench/main/job.yaml
kubectl wait --for=condition=complete job/kube-bench --timeout=120s
kubectl logs job/kube-bench > kube-bench-results.txt

# Parse results
grep -A 3 '\[FAIL\]' kube-bench-results.txt | head -50

# Specific checks to find:
# 1.2.1 - --anonymous-auth=false on API server
# 4.2.4 - --read-only-port=0 on kubelet
# 1.2.33 - etcd encryption at rest

cat kube-bench-results.txt | grep -A 5 'read-only-port'
cat kube-bench-results.txt | grep -A 5 'encryption-provider-config'
cat kube-bench-results.txt | grep -A 5 'anonymous-auth'
key_concepts: CIS Benchmarks are the industry standard for Kubernetes hardening — referenced by SOC2, PCI-DSS, HIPAA
kube-bench checks: API server flags, etcd config, kubelet settings, RBAC, CNI, addons
FAIL = definitively insecure; WARN = depends on context; PASS = compliant; INFO = manual check needed
minikube clusters will have more FAILs than production clusters (single-node, default config)
CIS Level 1 = practical hardening; Level 2 = maximum security (may impact functionality)
